headlines.geopolitics: Global Food Supply Faces a Dangerous Bottleneck as Iran War Persists
headlines.domestic: DOJ probes California, Maine over transgender inmate housing policies
headlines.economy: Diesel Is a Bigger Problem for Consumers Than Gasoline

metadata.daily_do: Check your local diesel prices before planning any major purchases this spring

clips.caption: When your wife catches you crying at Pixar movies 😭
Professor tries to understand modern dating apps
The most awkward Hinge date of all time

script.text: I matched with someone on Hinge whose job was literally to raise my rent. And I still went on the date. And it went well.
script.direction: Confessional, slightly embarrassed but also proud of the absurdity

script.text: March twenty-seventh. I'm Leo Barros. Global food prices are climbing as the Iran conflict disrupts fertilizer supplies from the region.
script.direction: Authoritative news anchor delivery, measured pace

script.text: Fertilizer costs have jumped eighteen percent in three weeks. The Middle East produces roughly forty percent of global potash and nitrogen fertilizer.
script.direction: Factual, building the story with specific data

script.text: When fertilizer gets expensive, farmers use less. When farmers use less, crop yields drop. When crop yields drop six months from now, grocery bills go up.
script.direction: Clear causal chain, slightly slower on each 'when' for emphasis

script.text: The Justice Department launched civil rights investigations into California and Maine prison systems over policies allowing transgender inmates in women's facilities.
script.direction: Neutral news tone, no editorial inflection

script.text: Attorney General Bondi cited allegations of sexual assault and what officials call a pervasive climate of sexual intimidation.
script.direction: Serious, reporting the official language precisely

script.text: The investigations target facilities where transgender women housed with female inmates allegedly committed assaults. California's policy stems from a twenty-twenty law. Maine officials say the probe is politically motivated.
script.direction: Balanced reporting, presenting both sides without taking one

script.text: Diesel prices jumped faster than gasoline this month, hitting trucking companies and businesses directly. Those costs flow straight into consumer prices.
script.direction: Economic story delivery, emphasis on 'straight into'

script.text: Everything you buy traveled on a diesel truck. When diesel costs more, everything costs more. It's that simple.
script.direction: Clear, declarative, making the connection obvious

script.text: Professor, food supply disruptions during wartime. Walk us through the pattern.
script.direction: Turning to Pringle with genuine curiosity, setting him up

script.text: Well Leo, there's a structural lesson here that appears in every major conflict since the Napoleonic Wars. Warfare doesn't just destroy armies. It destroys the mundane systems that keep civilization fed.
script.direction: Professorial, warming up to a topic he knows deeply

script.text: Napoleon's Continental System blocked British trade, yes, but it also cut off grain shipments across Europe. The Irish Potato Famine wasn't just about blight. It was about a food system so fragile that one crop failure cascaded into mass starvation.
script.direction: Building the historical argument with specific examples

script.text: World War One. Germany's unrestricted submarine warfare targeted merchant ships carrying food. The British nearly starved not from direct attack, but from supply chain collapse.
script.direction: Connecting past to present, slight emphasis on 'supply chain'

script.text: So you're saying this isn't about Iran targeting food supplies deliberately.
script.direction: Sharp follow-up question, pushing for clarity

script.text: Precisely. Modern warfare doesn't require sieges or blockades. Simply disrupting the industrial inputs that make modern agriculture possible achieves the same result.
script.direction: Satisfied that Leo understands, delivering the key insight

script.text: Fertilizer plants, shipping routes, processing facilities. These aren't military targets. They're economic infrastructure. But when they fail, the consequences are identical to deliberate starvation campaigns.
script.direction: Grave realization, letting the implications sink in

script.text: How long before those consequences reach American grocery stores?
script.direction: Direct question, getting to what listeners need to know

script.text: Agricultural economics suggests six to eight months from input disruption to retail price impact. We'll see this in October's harvest, January's grocery bills.
script.direction: Specific timeline, professor giving a direct answer

script.text: That's your Download. Now let's talk about what all of this actually means for real people. Jax, Duke, you've been listening.
script.direction: Professional transition, signaling the shift to a different tone

script.text: Okay first of all, hello everyone, I'm here now, the party can officially start.
script.direction: Playful entrance, announcing his arrival with mock fanfare

script.text: There he is.
script.direction: Dry, fond acknowledgment of Jax's dramatic entrance

script.text: But seriously, the fertilizer thing is wild because like, I had no idea we were one conflict away from expensive lettuce.
script.direction: Genuine reaction, processing the information in real time

script.text: Look, I've been watching diesel prices for weeks. My buddy runs a landscaping company and he's already raising prices because his trucks cost more to fill.
script.direction: Grounded perspective, connecting to real business impact

script.text: Jenny asked me why our grocery bill went up twenty bucks last week and I'm like, uh, Iran? She was not satisfied with that answer.
script.direction: Slightly exasperated, finding humor in domestic reality

script.text: Jenny's always right though. That's like, the first rule of marriage, right Professor?
script.direction: Turning to Pringle with genuine curiosity about marriage wisdom

script.text: Peggy certainly was. Though she would have said the first rule of marriage is picking up your shoes.
script.direction: Warm memory, slight smile in the voice

script.text: Oh man, the shoes thing is real. Jenny has like, a whole system for shoes by the door and I— look, I try, but apparently I have a blind spot for footwear organization.
script.direction: Admitting defeat, genuinely baffled by his own failing

script.text: The prison investigation is going to be interesting to watch. This cuts across a lot of different constituencies.
script.direction: Steering back toward serious analysis, journalist mode

script.text: Yeah, I mean, it's one of those stories where everyone's gonna have an opinion and nobody's gonna change their mind.
script.direction: Observational, slightly resigned about political discourse

script.text: The thing that gets me is we're talking about people who are incarcerated and vulnerable. Like, whatever your position is on the policy, everyone deserves to be safe in prison.
script.direction: Serious, grounded in basic human decency

script.text: Speaking of vulnerable people, I have to tell you guys something mortifying that happened to me this week.
script.direction: Transitioning to confessional mode, already slightly embarrassed

script.text: Oh no.
script.direction: Anticipating disaster, fond exasperation

script.text: So you know how I'm on the dating apps, right? I matched with someone on Hinge and her profile said policy analyst, which I thought sounded hot.
script.direction: Setting up the story, building anticipation

script.text: We get to the bar and it turns out she works for my landlord's property management company. Like, the actual company that's trying to raise my rent.
script.direction: Delivering the absurd twist, realizing how ridiculous it sounds

script.text: Oh dude, no.
script.direction: Secondhand embarrassment, but also amused

script.text: And here's the thing. I still went on the date. And it went well. I am now sleeping with the person who is literally pricing me out of my apartment.
script.direction: Confessional, can't believe his own choices

script.text: I must ask, what is a policy analyst?
script.direction: Genuinely curious, completely missing the romantic implications

script.text: In this case, Professor, it's someone who analyzes policies about how much to charge people for rent.
script.direction: Explaining patiently, finding humor in Pringle's innocence

script.text: Are you getting a rent discount?
script.direction: Dry, practical question cutting through the romantic drama

script.text: That would be unethical, Leo. I'm many things, but I'm not a monster.
script.direction: Mock indignation, pretending to have standards

script.text: You're dating someone whose job it is to make you homeless.
script.direction: Stating the obvious absurdity, deadpan delivery

script.text: When you put it like that, it sounds bad. But she's really smart and she has this thing where she explains housing policy while we're—
script.direction: Starting to defend himself, then realizing he's oversharing

script.text: And on that note.
script.direction: Quick intervention, cutting off Jax before he goes too far

script.text: That reminds me though, Jenny caught me crying at Inside Out Two last week.
script.direction: Transitioning to his own embarrassing story, matter-of-fact

script.text: Dude, that movie's emotional.
script.direction: Supportive, trying to normalize Duke's reaction

script.text: Not like a single dignified tear. Full ugly crying. The twins were fine. The three-year-old fell asleep. I was the only person in that theater who needed to be held.
script.direction: Admitting the full extent of his breakdown, slightly defensive

script.text: Jenny took a photo. She's saving it for what she calls leverage.
script.direction: Resigned to his fate, knowing he's been compromised

script.text: Smart woman.
script.direction: Appreciating Jenny's tactical thinking, amused

script.text: She is. Which is why I'm sleeping on the couch until I figure out how to get that photo deleted.
script.direction: Practical problem-solving, but clearly outmatched

script.text: I'm curious about these dating applications that Jackson uses. How does one analyze a policy while engaged in romantic— activities?
script.direction: Academic curiosity, genuinely trying to understand modern life

script.text: Professor, I'm gonna need you to never ask that question again.
script.direction: Mortified, but fond of Pringle's innocence

script.text: I merely wondered about the multitasking involved. Peggy and I never discussed municipal zoning during our— well, never mind.
script.direction: Trailing off as he realizes he's oversharing, slightly flustered

script.text: Professor, please don't finish that sentence.
script.direction: Cutting him off kindly but firmly, protecting everyone

script.text: I will tell you one thing about dating and then we are never discussing this topic again.
script.direction: Setting firm boundaries, but clearly has a story

script.text: Oh, here we go. What's her name?
script.direction: Excited to get gossip from Leo, leaning in

script.text: I went on a date last month and she googled me at the table. While I was sitting right there. She read my Wikipedia page out loud to me.
script.direction: Still processing the audacity of it, genuinely baffled

script.text: That's rough, man.
script.direction: Sympathetic, recognizing how uncomfortable that would be

script.text: Quote, it says here you left NBC under controversial circumstances, end quote. I said check please.
script.direction: Recounting her exact words, still annoyed by the memory

script.text: Did you at least get dinner first?
script.direction: Looking for the silver lining, trying to lighten the mood

script.text: We hadn't even ordered appetizers.
script.direction: The final insult, delivered with resigned frustration

script.text: My granddaughter informed me last week that I had, quote, thirst-trapped Historian Twitter because she posted a photograph of me in my garden without my knowledge.
script.direction: Bewildered by the terminology but reporting the facts

script.text: Wait, what?
script.direction: Delighted by this revelation, needing more details

script.text: Apparently I was wearing a sun hat and that was somehow appealing to the academic community. I received three hundred messages. I understand none of this.
script.direction: Completely out of his depth, reporting strange phenomena

script.text: Professor, you went viral.
script.direction: Explaining modern internet culture with patience

script.text: Like a disease?
script.direction: Genuinely concerned, taking the term literally

script.text: No Professor, like famous. You're famous for being hot.
script.direction: Trying to explain internet fame, barely containing laughter

script.text: I was gardening. It was eighty-seven degrees.
script.direction: Confused, stating the obvious practical reason for his appearance

script.text: Professor, you have admirers.
script.direction: Gently explaining the situation, trying not to embarrass him

script.text: Peggy would have found this hilarious. She always said I looked distinguished in that hat.
script.direction: Sweet memory, connecting to his late wife with affection

script.text: Okay but real talk, are we all just disasters at relationships? Like, what is happening here?
script.direction: Taking stock of everyone's romantic failures, slightly concerned

script.text: I mean, speak for yourself. I'm crushing it. My landlord's employee is very satisfied with my performance.
script.direction: Defensive pride, missing Duke's point entirely

script.text: That came out wrong.
script.direction: Pointing out the unintended innuendo, dry observation

script.text: Did it though?
script.direction: Realizing the double meaning, leaning into it shamelessly

script.text: And on that note, let's talk about something else. Literally anything else.
script.direction: Desperately changing the subject, taking Leo's usual role

script.text: Okay, weird news time. Nepal's new Prime Minister gave his first address to the nation as a rap song.
script.direction: Shifting to lighter material, energetic delivery

script.text: I'm sorry, what?
script.direction: Double-taking, needing confirmation he heard correctly

script.text: Full rap. Bars about economic policy and infrastructure development. It's actually not terrible.
script.direction: Genuinely impressed despite the absurdity

script.text: Poetry has always been a vehicle for political discourse. Homer's Iliad was essentially foreign policy analysis in verse.
script.direction: Finding the historical precedent, taking it seriously

script.text: Only you could make Nepalese rap sound scholarly, Professor.
script.direction: Admiring Pringle's ability to find depth anywhere

script.text: Also, Florida Man tried to protect his car during a hailstorm by lying on top of it. Which, honestly, I respect the commitment.
script.direction: Appreciating the dedication despite the stupidity

script.text: That's either the dumbest thing I've ever heard or the most devoted car owner in America.
script.direction: Torn between mockery and admiration

script.text: Maybe both? Like, insurance exists, but also, that's his car, you know?
script.direction: Finding unexpected logic in the madness

script.text: The Florida Man stories never stop being Florida Man stories.
script.direction: Resigned acceptance of Florida's eternal chaos

script.text: And finally, someone in Jackson is complaining about their neighbor's petting zoo slash synagogue combo that's operating quote, illegally, across the street.
script.direction: Building up to the absurd combination, enjoying the weirdness

script.text: I'm not certain I understand the problem. Synagogues have always been community gathering places.
script.direction: Genuinely puzzled, applying historical context

script.text: I think the issue is the goats, Professor.
script.direction: Gently explaining the practical problem

script.text: Ah. Zoning regulations.
script.direction: Understanding dawning, nodding sagely

script.text: Right? Like, I support religious freedom and I love petting zoos, but maybe not in the same building?
script.direction: Trying to be diplomatic about an inherently ridiculous situation

script.text: Alright, your Daily Do. Check diesel prices at gas stations near you before making any major purchases this spring.
script.direction: Back to practical advice, clear and actionable

script.text: If diesel's up significantly, expect delivery fees and shipping costs to climb in the next few weeks.
script.direction: Connecting the economic dots for listeners

script.text: As the Roman historian Tacitus wrote, the sinews of war are infinite money. Today, the sinews of commerce are affordable fuel.
script.direction: Thoughtful closing wisdom, connecting ancient and modern

script.text: And remember, if you're gonna date someone who controls your housing costs, at least negotiate first. I'm Jax.
script.direction: Self-deprecating callback to his own story, owning the absurdity

script.text: Duke.
script.direction: Simple sign-off, still processing everything that just happened

script.text: Professor Pringle. And please, stop sending me photographs on the Twitter.
script.direction: Polite but bewildered plea to his newfound internet fame

script.text: Leo Barros. We'll see you tomorrow on The Friction.
script.direction: Professional close, bringing the show home
